pages.title: How Levels Are Decided
pages.text: When a mob spawns, the system follows a strict hierarchy to determine its level. Think of it as layers of influence-the most specific location rules always take precedence.$(br2)$(br)The order is absolute:$(br)$(bold)Structure > Biome > Base Rules > Defaults

pages.title: Tier 1: Structure Rules
pages.text: $(bold)Structure rules$() are the highest authority. When you enter an ancient stronghold, a nether fortress, or an end city, the structure's power overrides everything else.$(br2)$(li)Stronghold mobs may all be level 50+$(li)Nether fortresses could have minimum level 30$(li)End cities might spawn level 100 enemies$(br2)$(o)Structures represent concentrated danger zones.

pages.title: Tier 2: Biome Rules
pages.text: If no structure claims the area, $(bold)biome rules$() apply. The Deep Dark, The Nether, and The End naturally harbor stronger creatures than peaceful plains or forests.$(br2)Biome rules stack with distance scaling-venturing far into dangerous biomes creates truly terrifying encounters.$(br2)$(o)Some biomes may have minimum levels regardless of distance.

pages.title: Tier 3: Base & Default Rules
pages.text: With no structure or biome rule, $(bold)base rules$() apply based on mob type. Hostile mobs, passive animals, and bosses can each have their own scaling patterns.$(br2)Finally, if nothing else applies, distance-based scaling from world configuration takes over-starting from default minimum and growing as you travel outward.$(br2)$(o)The world truly becomes more dangerous the further you roam.
